DecodeLabs Internship 2026 — Project 04

The Living Prototype

From the soil. To your door.

Static screens tell stakeholders what the app looks like. A prototype tells them how it feels. Project 4 bridges the gap between design and reality through transitions, micro-interactions, and a complete clickable user flow.

App Rooted — Organic Food Delivery
Screens 6 Connected Screens
Tool Figma Prototype Mode
Frame iPhone 14 Pro
Try the Prototype →

The Brief

Making the Design Feel Real

Stakeholders can't approve what they can't feel. The final project takes the Rooted visual identity and breathes life into it — connecting all six screens with intentional transitions and micro-interactions that make the app behave like a real product.

The Challenge

Bridge the gap between static hi-fi screens and a real app experience. Every interaction must feel native, intentional, and consistent — not animated for its own sake.

Smart Animate Push Transitions Micro-Interactions

The Approach

Wire the complete happy path — Splash to Confirmation — using Figma's prototype tools. Add button state micro-interactions at the two highest-stakes moments: Add to Cart and Place Order.

6 Screens 9 Connections 2 Micro-Interactions

The Happy Path

Six Screens, One Journey

Every screen has a clear job in the user journey. Nothing dead-ends. Every forward action has a corresponding back navigation except the Confirmation screen, which closes the loop back to shopping.

01 Splash
→
02 Product List
→
03 Product Detail
→
04 Cart
→
05 Checkout
→
06 Confirmation

Screens 01 — 03

Discovery to Decision

The first half of the flow moves the user from brand introduction through product discovery to a committed purchase intent. Each screen narrows focus.

01 — Splash

Full-bleed image, Rooted brand mark, single Get Started CTA. Transition out: Push Up, 350ms ease out.

02 — Product List

Search, category filters, 2-column product grid. Transition in: Push Left, 300ms ease out.

03 — Product Detail

Hero image, certifications, farm origin, Add to Cart micro-interaction. Transition in: Push Left, 300ms ease out.

Screens 04 — 06

Commitment to Confirmation

The second half of the flow handles the transaction. Every friction point — surprise fees, unclear totals, ambiguous CTAs — was eliminated before prototyping began.

04 — Cart

Item review, quantity controls, subtotal, delivery fee, total. Proceed to Checkout CTA. Transition in: Push Left, 300ms ease out.

05 — Checkout

Delivery address, payment method selection, expanded order summary. Place Order micro-interaction with loading state. Transition in: Push Left, 300ms ease out.

06 — Confirmation

Harvest Gold success mark, order number, full receipt breakdown. Continue Shopping returns to Product List. Transition in: Dissolve, 400ms ease in and out.

Interaction Specification

Every Connection Documented

Prototype connections aren't guesswork. Every transition has a direction, easing, and duration chosen for a specific reason — not because it looked cool in the animation picker.

From To Trigger Animation Easing Duration Why
Splash Product List Get Started tap Push Up Ease out 350ms App launching from below — entry feels purposeful
Product List Product Detail Product card tap Push Left Ease out 300ms Standard iOS forward navigation
Product Detail Product Detail — Added Add to Cart tap Smart Animate Ease out 200ms Button morphs to success state before navigating
Product Detail — Added Cart After delay 800ms Push Left Ease out 300ms Auto-advance after success state registers
Cart Checkout Proceed to Checkout tap Push Left Ease out 300ms Standard iOS forward navigation
Checkout Checkout — Loading Place Order tap Smart Animate Ease out 200ms Button shows Harvest Gold spinner — processing feedback
Checkout — Loading Confirmation After delay 1000ms Dissolve Ease in and out 400ms Dissolve signals completion, not navigation
Confirmation Product List Continue Shopping tap Push Left Ease out 300ms Closes the loop, returns to browse
Any screen Previous screen Back arrow tap Push Right Ease out 300ms Mirror of forward navigation — native iOS back
Design Principle

We don't just design screens. We design behaviour.

The brief's closing line is the most important thing in it. A prototype isn't a decoration on top of design work — it's the proof that the design actually makes sense as a system. If the transitions feel wrong, the layout is wrong. If the user gets lost, the hierarchy is wrong. Prototyping isn't the final step. It's the test that validates everything that came before it.
